title: Beijing’s Campaign Against Overcapacity Creates Private Credit Opportunities
dek: China’s renewed campaign against industrial overcapacity is likely to create multi-year demand for restructuring expertise, asset-backed lending, and distressed-asset solutions.
keyFindings: ShoreVest’s private credit strategy is built around asset-backed lending, asset restructuring, and distressed debt investment.
China’s overcapacity campaign is moving from policy signaling toward enforcement.
Sunset and sunrise industries both face consolidation pressure, but cost allocation remains the central challenge.
Restructuring and asset-disposal needs may expand demand for specialized private credit capabilities.

sections.heading: Why overcapacity matters now
paragraphs: Overcapacity is one of China’s most pressing macroeconomic challenges. It has contributed to entrenched producer-price deflation, weaker corporate profitability, and declining government tax revenues.

In response, Beijing has elevated overcapacity reduction to a top policy priority. The campaign remains in its early stages, with unresolved questions around who should absorb closure costs, how quickly excess capacity should be removed, and how distressed or underutilized assets should be repurposed.

Periods of deleveraging, economic transition, and stabilization tend to generate significant private credit opportunities. China’s current transition away from legacy growth drivers is likely the beginning of a multi-year process requiring creative financing and restructuring expertise.

sections.heading: Sunset and sunrise overcapacity
paragraphs: Overcapacity can be grouped into sunset industries tied to the former housing-led growth model and sunrise industries that Beijing aims to promote. Sunset sectors include steel, flat glass, cement, home appliances, and construction machinery. Demand in these sectors is unlikely to return to pre-2021 peak levels.

Sunrise sectors include electric vehicles, batteries, and solar panels. Overcapacity in these areas has been driven by broad local-government support for expansion and corporate strategies focused on market share over near-term profits. Local support has delayed market exits, so industrial consolidation has moved more slowly than expected.

sections.heading: From signaling to enforcement
paragraphs: Beijing first identified overcapacity as a national challenge at the Central Economic Work Conference in December 2023. Early policy implementation was limited, and producer prices continued to decline. A stronger intervention phase began in late June and early July.

Policy statements criticized local government subsidy races, duplicative investment, local protectionism, and disruptive price wars, while emphasizing orderly exit of outdated capacity and accountability for local officials. By August, fixed-asset investment had declined in sectors associated with targeted overcapacity reduction.

sections.heading: Who pays for capacity exit?
paragraphs: The central practical issue is cost allocation. Potential bearers include local governments, equity holders, creditors, workers, and hybrid burden-sharing structures. Polysilicon offers a live case study, with industry participants discussing a large fund to acquire outdated smaller plants and reduce capacity.

Even a meaningful capacity cut would remain above demand but could help stabilize pricing. Negotiations are complex, particularly around contribution formulas and asset pricing, and a target for agreement has likely slipped into 2026.

sections.heading: Private credit implications
paragraphs: A broad overcapacity campaign is likely to expand demand for mergers and acquisitions consolidation, corporate restructuring, distressed asset disposal, and collateral repurposing.

Banks will participate, but many transactions require specialized credit structuring and operational restructuring capabilities—areas where private credit investors can play a differentiated role. What began as overcapacity concerns concentrated in cleantech may widen into a larger economy-wide restructuring cycle.
